title: Grape Extracts or Phytochemicals as Ingredients in Cosmetics. Plant-derived ingredients have been a cornerstone of skincare for hundreds of years. This applies to grapes too, which are used as skin-conditioning agents, antioxidants, fragrances, and colorants in cosmetics. According to the CosIng database, which is a European Commission database for information on cosmetic substances and ingredients, the fruits and roots of grapes are utilized as skin-conditioning agents, while the seeds are used for their skin protection, skin-conditioning, antibacterial, antioxidant, and anti-seborrheic properties. The grape seed oil has emollient properties, whereas the stem is used for its antioxidant and photoprotection properties. Grape leaves are highly valued in cosmetic formulations as a source of fragrance.

sentences.text: Grape Extracts or Phytochemicals as Ingredients in Cosmetics.
Plant-derived ingredients have been a cornerstone of skincare for hundreds of years.
This applies to grapes too, which are used as skin-conditioning agents, antioxidants, fragrances, and colorants in cosmetics.

According to the CosIng database, which is a European Commission database for information on cosmetic substances and ingredients, the fruits and roots of grapes are utilized as skin-conditioning agents, while the seeds are used for their skin protection, skin-conditioning, antibacterial, antioxidant, and anti-seborrheic properties.
The grape seed oil has emollient properties, whereas the stem is used for its antioxidant and photoprotection properties.
Grape leaves are highly valued in cosmetic formulations as a source of fragrance.

Grapes are particularly valued for their mildness and minimal skin-irritating properties, making them ideal for sensitive skin formulations for potential skin benefits, including antiaging, photoprotection, and antioxidative properties.
Since, in general, cosmetic products must be tested for safety before they can be marketed, efforts have been made to ensure the safety of grape extracts and compounds.
The cytotoxicity and skin compatibility of grape extracts have been validated in vitro in numerous investigations.
For example, it has been demonstrated that formulations containing grape extracts do not cause cytotoxicity to keratinocytes and fibroblasts, suggesting their suitability for topical formulations.
Further, clinical research has shown that formulations containing grape polyphenols can increase skin moisture and minimize wrinkles without causing adverse side effects.

The Cosmetic Ingredient Review Expert Panel evaluated the safety of 24 grape-derived ingredients in cosmetics and concluded that they are safe for use in their currently used concentrations below 1 to 3 percent.
In a dermal irritation test on human skin, extracts from grape fruit, juice, and seeds at concentrations up to 1 to 3 percent showed no signs of irritation or sensitization.
Similarly, a two-week human study involving products with 0.15 percent grape seed extract demonstrated no irritation.

The Cosmetic Ingredient Review's safety assessments were based on available clinical and animal data, as well as historical safety evaluations of grape-derived constituents such as resveratrol and other polyphenols.
These findings provide a foundation for the continued use of grape-based ingredients in cosmetic formulations.

Grape seed powder, seed extract, seed oil, skin extract, stem extract, wine lees, pomace, and isolated resveratrol and flavonoids are readily incorporated into a wide range of skincare products such as serums, creams, masks, toners, hair oils, oral care products, and sunscreens.
